segments.text: December, six weeks ago, along with haifu. And so we welcome you too, Mr. Rennie as Secretary of the Treasury. There's a lot of speculation at breakfast TV that there'll be another announcement this morning, so we look forward to that too, and we'll first open it with introductory remarks, and then we'll have first comment and question from the honourable Barbara Edmunds. So welcome Minister.

Thank you very much, Mr. Chair and Good morning colleagues alongside me from the Treasury are Ian Rennie, the secretary, and Chris bunny, who is Deputy Secretary budget and fiscal performance, a key purpose of the Budget Policy Statement, which we are discussing today is to preview the upcoming budget, and I am happy to announce that the budget this year will be on the 28th of May, and as has been the case with this government, it will be a responsible budget, and It will be a budget that invests in the important things and makes savings to ensure that we can do important things in the future. This year's budget will focus, therefore, on supporting the delivery of core public services, in particular, investments for healthcare, investments for education, for rebuilding the capacity of our defence force, and in our law and order protections the budget in order to make these investments will demonstrate tight control of discretionary government spending and will fund only a limited number of commitments. There will be no splashing the cash that would be an irresponsible course of action given the several years in which New Zealand has previously been building up debt and deficit. Instead, our budget will show careful stewardship of public finances outside the priority areas I just mentioned, most agencies and most ministers have been asked to plan to manage pressures with little or no additional funding. We are expecting them to act as households do, which is to say, in order to keep investments going and the important things like our children's education and health care, we will need to make sure that we are not making big discretionary increases in spending elsewhere. Our government is running a tight ship. This tight ship, of course, has allowed us to ensure that tax relief continues to flow into New Zealanders bank accounts, and that the investment boost tax relief for businesses continues to operate, fixing a basic aspect of our tax law now, but also building a more productive future for New Zealand. Over the last two budgets, we've made cumulative ongoing savings of more than $11 billion a year. These savings have allowed us to maintain investment in frontline services, while at the same time plotting the course back to balanced books to surplus and declining debt. So it will be no surprise to anyone that savings and reprioritisation will again be key features of this budget, I want to acknowledge that these sorts of decisions are always tough. They are also necessary to get back the books back in order. In the absence of this decision, we would either have to not make critical investments in health, education, defence, our police or we would have to sign up future New Zealanders for unsustainable levels of debt. Now, of course, the other purpose of the budget policy statement is to look at how the government's fiscal strategy is tracking against the Treasury's latest forecasts. In an absolute sense, the forecasts tell a very positive story, with the economy clearly picking up, the forecasts show GDP growth and tax revenue strengthening over the next year and beyond, alongside our disciplined approach to spending, that means the government's headline operating balance measure, obergs, is expected to return to surplus in the four year forecast period, and the debt curve bends from going up to going down, which is the way we want it going. That's the absolute story in the forecasts. There's always also a relative story, and that is the pickup is expected to be slightly slower than was forecast last year at the budget update, I am happy to comment members more on what recent upticks and economic activity mean for these forecasts. You can ask me questions about that. The Oberg X track is therefore slightly lower than at the budget update last year, reaching surplus and 2930 fiscal year a year later than previously expected. In that is simply a forecast rescission decision. It is not the result any government policy changes, forecast revisions of the sort happen at every forecast. Of course, we have had a series of these downward revisions which reflect both uncertainty about how the downturn would play out and the unwinding of some highly optimistic assumptions made prior to the 2023 election by the Treasury at that time. And look, obviously these forecast revisions are head ones, but the government has repeatedly said it won't overreact to forecast changes. We have maintained very prudent operating allowances, as we said we would. We've taken a deliberate medium term approach to fiscal consolidation that minimises the impact on individuals and on critical public services, but also shows a credible path to surplus and debt reducing as a proportion of the economy. I'll acknowledge some argue for a more aggressive consolidation, and we need to be very upfront that that would involve either deep spending reductions or big tax increases. Others, of course, oppose every single savings measure the government has undertaken but necessary tough choices we have made. Well that course, would see debt spiral out of control. So I'm happy that our government is getting the balance right. Also, caution against making too much of the relatively small differences between very large numbers that are forecast years in advance. It's not possible to finely tune those numbers, to give you some context, and 2829 for example, total crown revenue is forecast to be just over 200 billion, 200 billion, and total crown expenses are forecast to be just over 200 billion. Those are two very large numbers, and the difference is less than a billion. And at some stage, of course, also we will be getting upside surprises in the forecasts. And on that happy note, I'm available to take the committee's questions. Oh,
thank you very much. Minister, I'm going to go to the honourable Barbara Edmonds.

Happy New Year. Minister, welcome back in the BPS appendix Treasury shows the change in real GDP forecast between pre foo, 23 and haifu, 25 at each update, the forecast GDP figure for the calendar year 26 has fallen from 304 billion at pre fu To 289 billion at haifu. That's a fall of 4.7% of GDP. What responsibility do you have for that decline?

Well, look, as the member understands as I think every New Zealander has experienced, the economic recovery did not come as quickly as had been forecast by the Treasury, which led to them immediately in their first set of forecasts they provided to me after the election, downgrading a number of their forecasts, but also revising some of their assumptions. And you've seen that in the way that the numbers have trapped over time. And of course, what that reflects is the fact that the economy has struggled to come back from a period of very high inflation, 11 official cash rate rises in a row, and it has been the government's position that it is our responsibility to, through discipline spending, take pressure off inflation that has occurred, to allow the Reserve Bank space to reduce The Official Cash Rate that has occurred with nine reductions so far, and that in that environment, growth is returning, and we are seeing that now. But look, I don't think it's a surprise to anyone that that recovery has not come as soon or as fast as forecasters had originally anticipated.
So how much of that 4.7% of GDP decline do you put down to forecasting?

It simply is a result of the fact that the economy has not grown as fast as was forecast, and I have not been advised that the government, through any of its specific policy choices, has contributed to that decline. In fact, the Treasury's advice is that a number of the measures we have taken have supported GDP to be stronger than it would otherwise have been. A key example of that, of course, is that in the absence of discipline spending, we would be in a situation where the reserve bank wouldn't have had the space to reduce the official cash rate as much, meaning that interest rates would be higher. Economic activity wouldn't be more constrained. It's also the case than the absence of tax relief, the real income of New Zealanders right now would be significantly lower.

So your work. Words in relation to treasuries forecast at pre Fu. So I can't remember. It wasn't ambitious. It wasn't was it overly optimistic? I think it is. Do you have confidence in treasuries forecasts, given that they say you will be in surplus during this forecast period under obergale X, new measure,

I do have confidence in their forecasts. One of the things I'd point out is that it wasn't just Treasury who made overly optimistic assumptions at the time of the pre election update, if you look across what the key bank economists were saying at that time, a number of commentators on the New Zealand economy, there was a presumption that the bounce back from the covid period would be faster than it was. In a sense, immediately following the covid crisis and the soaring inflation, there was a sense that that would correct itself quicker than it did. So treasury was not alone. They have been quick to revise their assumptions as the real data has come in and indicated that bouncing back from something as major as covid with the huge amounts of fiscal stimulus that has occurred has been harder for the economy than had been anticipated.

Minister, would you be able to provide the committee? Obviously, we don't have it on you here today, the advice from Treasury that stated it was overly optimistic and it was not due to any government decisions.

Overly optimistic is my phrase. I'm very happy to provide you advice from the Treasury about why they revise their forecast assumptions and what they think they got wrong prior to the election. So I've had extensive advice on that. You can imagine coming in as a minister, having been presented a set of forecasts prior to the election with the fair expectation that that is what would happen to immediately on becoming the minister, have significant downward revisions, has been something that I have interrogated quite deeply. Yeah, 4.7% of GDP fall in in this forecast period since prefer, that's right. And I think the point that I'm making to you, and that you'll understand, is that it wouldn't matter if it was me sitting at the desk, if it was Grant Robertson sitting at the desk, or Chloe Swarbrick sitting at the desk, those revisions to forecasts would have occurred on anyone's watch. And I think the fear question for the government is, how do you respond to that? And of course, we did have a choice. Some would say at that point you should have made far deeper savings by making significant reductions in public services. Others would have said you shouldn't have provided any tax relief to those really struggling with the cost of living crisis who were in need of that relief, our approach has been to say, actually, we shouldn't overreact to these forecasts. There is value and having stability in the way that we manage that spending to ensure that we are making the investments needed and critical services that are actually good for future growth, while also ensuring that we do take tough calls to make sure that we keep to very low operating allowances. The key fiscal decision which we have faced is how big those operating allowances will be, and we have actually spent less discretionary money in those operating allowances than the national policy campaigned on. We have consistently delivered lower spending than the previous government did, and top ups to its budgets.
So speaking of your savings, do you stand by your statements that you made continually through the end of 2023 and 24 less so in 2025 that you will not make cut cuts to frontline services?

Yes, as is evidenced by our actions, we have made significant investments in education, in health, in our police, in our defence system, and it is going to continue to be the case. People around this table have heard these debates for years. I expect that some of my opposition colleagues will continue to paint decisions that we make as as cuts to frontline services. Your definitions will vary from mine, but I'm proud of the fact health spending as proportion of the economy is now much higher than it was that we have made investments not just in essential education services, but in our special education service that we're taking on the really difficult task of rebuilding investment and defence after years and years of decline that we have invested in more police for the front line. And so our approach is to say, what are the things that are critical, both now and for our future as a country, and what are the things that actually we need to look ourselves in the mirror about and say we probably can't afford this anymore.
So therefore, will you commit to in budget 2026 that in the core areas of health, education and social services, that operating costs will keep up with inflation and with population growth?
Well in each of those areas. Areas, we will be making investments to meet cost pressures that exist.

Sorry. And last question, Minister, just in relation to, for example, say, education, sorry, health, you've talked about potentially looking at changing the way the cost pressures is over three years, will you commit to actually hiring all the graduate nurses that we have here in New Zealand, given last year, it showed that you had not hired every single nurse

in terms of your former point. I just want to be very clear with members the government has already pre committed that we will be locking in an operating increase to the health New Zealand budget next year at $1.4 billion that is not changing. We've made that commitment to health New Zealand so that they can plan with confidence and that that is actually a number that was originally derived based on inflation and demographic assumptions that Treasury made, and we are sticking to that. In terms of nurses, I point out to the member that under our government, around 2000 additional nurses have been hired across the public health system, and around 500 additional doctors now health New Zealand must maintain some discretion about which graduates they hire, and the Minister of Health will have more to say about the directions he's giving to health New Zealand about hiring new nurses.

Okay, we'll leave it at that. We're going to go to Chloe Warwick morning, a minister. Welcome to election year. Can I just ask first and foremost, if you agree with the fact that government decisions to increase climate changing emissions contributes to more and more frequent and more intense climate change charge weather events and therefore reduces our financial resilience and stability.

Well, the first thing to make clear is that our government is committed to emission reduction and is meeting our obligations under the zero carbon Act, a piece of legislation that has been supported by members across this house our emission reduction budgets are on track to achieve the target set out in that zero carbon law. The second thing to acknowledge is that New Zealand globally contributes an absolutely tiny proportion of overall emissions. And the reason this is relevant is that we do need to, I think, acknowledge as sensible members that even if New Zealand was to cut its emissions to zero, the impact of significant emissions from larger emitters overseas at the levels they currently are in, a forecast to be is such that scientists predict there would still be significant climatic events occurring into the future. So I believe the prudent position is for New Zealand to do two things at once. On the one hand, to take sensible steps to reduce our own emissions, on the other hand, to prepare for the likely reality that we will see more extreme climatic events in the future, no matter what good actions we take here at home, and that we need to prepare for that reality. I'm really

glad that you raised the point of preparation, because I wanted to ask if you stand by your decision to scrap $20 million from climate resilience and preparation and adaptation projects in budget 2024 like, for example, the Maori Climate Resilience Project, which would have allowed the very marae that the Prime Minister has been celebrating and lauding this past week for their climate emergency response.

Yes, Chloe, I do stand by that. We did a line by line to assess which things were actually making a difference. And our government has made significant investments in marae and in resilience. Through the regional infrastructure fund, we have made contributions of millions of dollars to marae facilities at Ratana. Just last week, we announced $10 million from marae facilities in the Bay of Plenty associated with the Rangi tou church movement. Through tua ni Ko kiriri, we have maintained investments and marae community responses. Through the regional infrastructure fund, we have invested hundreds of millions of dollars in specific flood resilience projects, including stock banks, culvert works, we've partnered with local government to provide them additional funding to do that work. So the good work of investing in climate resilience has continued. We have worked to do that in the most effective way possible.

Sorry, just to clarify, and if I can have just a short answer to this piece in particular, are you saying, or can you confirm, rather, that your Government has cut funding that otherwise would have gone towards adaptation and resilience projects, you are investing less money in climate adaptation and resilience? No, I

can't confirm that. What I can confirm is what was set out clearly in the budget documents, in budget 2024, and in the pre budget announcement, and at the end of 2023 where we identified a number of itemised particular programmes or items of expenditure that we didn't believe met the effectiveness test that we stopped. We stand by those decisions. My point is simply that the government has also, at the same time made Q. Cumulative decisions to invest in other resilience initiatives,

which external analysis and your own official advisors say will increase climate changing emissions and has resulted in billions of dollars being cut from climate and environmental projects. But nonetheless, do you stand by your decision to cut billions of dollars from the climate Emergency Response Fund and to channel that into generalised tax

cuts? I'd like to make two points on that, Mr. Chair. The first is $2.8 billion of that fund was directly invested in our response to cyclone Gabrielle, the Auckland floods, Cyclone Hale that included significant investments, particularly in roading infrastructure, but also in measures to remove silt, debris and the like going forward. The position of our government has been that, of course, there will continue, unfortunately, to be severe climatic events, as we have seen in recent days. And of course, the government needs to stand ready to respond to those, rather than having a separate add on to the side situation. We have made that integral to our infrastructure planning and funding. So for example, the new ng ta, as a result of our government policy statement in 2024 set out specific funds available both for rebuilding state highways following major events, and for rebuilding local roads following major events, they also have underway a bespoke resilience programme, which has the purpose of identifying roads that are likely to be particularly affected by, for example, sea level rise, land slips, flooding, and to make those roads more resilient to those so our focus has been on funding more resilient infrastructure via the existing tools and mechanisms that we have to allow the right experts the right officials to prioritise the activity that is needed.

Does it alarm you that a third of the emissions reduction plan that you are referring to in your initial answer to me relies on carbon capture technology, which the industry itself has said is completely economically unfeasible without government intervention and support.

No, I'm excited by what science can offer as solutions to our climate challenges as a world and carbon capture to me, I recognise there are some who wish to bury their heads in the sand and say, No, we just need to stop using all carbon. But actually, carbon capture allows us a way in which we can continue to have the benefits of growth and energy use, but also reduce the emissions from carbon. So I think that that is it's positive that carbon capture is envisaged in
our mission just one just one more, and then

respect. Respectfully, Minister, that wasn't my question. My question was about the fact that the industry of cut that is responsible for carbon capture technologies that you are saying can result in all of this magical, mythical stuff happening is saying that this is completely economically unfeasible based on the hard economic facts, unless there is to be active government support. Your government is making decisions right now, per budget last year, to subsidise new fossil fuel production to the tune of $200 million of taxpayer money, but you are not investing in carbon capture technology, which you are relying for fully a third of in your emissions reduction plan, and you're simply saying that this is awesome, but not showing us the money.

Well, there's a number of problems with the statements that you've just made. The first is, of course, guess what? Industry will always say we'd like money from the government to do this or do that. That doesn't mean necessarily that we always need to respond to the requests for additional subsidies. The second thing is that our emissions reduction plan is put together with extensive advice and oversight and critique from officials, and they have judged that the emissions reduction plan is plausible and is feasible.
Can
I just take a close fall break up on this matter, you keep saying that we're investing 200 million in fossil fuels. I think what you are referring to is the is that
your
co investment

is that we have committed that we will help ensure that New Zealand has security of fuel supply in times when the rain isn't sufficient for our hydro generation, and when the when the sun isn't shining enough and the wind isn't blowing, so that we can keep the lights on, and that this may involve the importation of LNG, to describe that as a subsidy for the fossil
industry. Because we're in the second half of this hearing, and we've, we're on Speaker Number two, and there's a long list here. I'm going to go to a sup for Dr Russell. No, no,
I'll hand it to me. And because Chloe asked my question,
okay,
sorry, just on the comment you made there, Minister, the $200 million co Investment Fund for new gas fields, you just said that would
be, oh, sorry, you were referring to new gas fields. No. Sorry, I thought you were referring to the l ng, no,
no, so I was going to ask you, are you saying now that? So you're not saying that that $200 million fund is going to be part of the l ng,
no, that's not what I'm saying at all. I'm characterising what that is that policy is about differently from you. You seem to want a future in which the coal mountain grows ever bigger.
No, I don't.
I recognise the gas has half the emissions. Okay?

Yeah, I'm just wanting clarity. So the $200 million fund that was announced in budget 2035 for Minister Jones in terms of CO investment and gas fields, confirming that that will not extend to NG. Just a simple question,
no, I want
to thank you.

Okay, before I go to Todd Stevenson, I just just want to get confirmation from you today, Minister, that out of your $2.4 billion operating allowance, you're saying to us today that 1.4 billion will be going to health, and that is new and additional money on the budget come 28

mai our budget that the lion's share of new investment will go to the New Zealand Health Service, and that reflects our priority as a government in which we have set clear targets to deliver health services more effectively, to reduce waiting lists, ensure people can get elective surgery, that immunisation races, that waiting times and emergency rooms reduce, and that does require significant investment, and so the bulk of this year's budget will be investment in the New Zealand Health
Service.
Okay, thank you. Todd Stevenson,

thank you morning minister. Happy 2026 I just want to talk a little bit about in the budget policy statement, the goals and priorities that you've set out as a government, and then just also referencing some of your comments this morning. So obviously you've got it sets out three overarching goals here around building a more productive economy that lifts incomes and increased opportunities. We've got this more efficient and effective, responsive public services, which one I'm particularly interested in, and then we've obviously got the government getting its books back into order in this fiscal discipline. And again, you mentioned today, you know, tight control of discretionary government spending, little or no additional spending in each kind of, I suppose, department and then savings and re prioritisation. What does you know, looking at these goals, and then I'm imagining you're having quite robust conversations with some of your ministerial colleagues. So can you just tell me about how your Yeah, just some of your thoughts, preparing the budget and then having some of those discussions, because and then how you might even evaluate some of the proposals that are coming forward against those goals
and

priorities? Yeah. Look, it is the case of a Minister of Finance who's working to run a tight ship that you learn the word no, and you use the word no, because if I say yes to every single thing that has put its hand up and said, we could use some more money in this. We could use some money in that, then what I would be delivering to New Zealand is an unsustainable level of spending and debt, and that would be reckless. What we've asked every government agency to do is prepare a performance plan annually. And these performance plans must set out what pressures they see on their ability to deliver frontline services in terms of the financial resources, to set out a plan for how they envisage re jigging resources within their current budgets in order to meet those pressures to evaluate which programmes or lines of activity are not as efficient as they could be. Could be done better using new technology, using data systems. And so it is our expectation that for every government agency, every government minister should constantly be in the process of saying, How can we do more with less? There's

a quick supplementary and then if colleagues brought forward, are you open to the idea of also basically moving money between different ministries and departments? If a colleague could say, well, actually, if you give me a few extra dollars, I can save another area money. Are they the kinds of discussions you're hoping to have? And again, I suppose it comes back to your social investment approach, in a way to minister so very keen to see if you're having those kind of conversations.

Absolutely, it is the case that there are what I call spend to save initiatives, which is to say, sometimes spending a little bit up front can deliver dividends down the line. Now, Treasury, rightly, are very rigorous about evaluating those assertions. They need to see that there will be tangible results within the forecast period, that there is evidence to really outline that those savings will occur before they'll just book it in, as we hope, there'll be some savings in the future. Good example of that is the upfront investment we made through MSD to ensure better housing, transitional support for people who would otherwise be in emergency housing. The case to us was, if you make sure we have frontline resources in this area, we can lower your motel bill from what was a million dollars a day that has occurred. Heard, I am really proud of the fact that we have significantly, massively reduced the number of New Zealanders living in inappropriate motel accommodation. We've got kids out of skanky motel rooms and interstate homes, and we have done that both to save money also to deliver them better lives. And yes, sometimes that involves an upfront investment. More broadly, in the social investment portfolio, we're increasingly working to ask agencies to work together to pull money so that we can take a whole of government, whole of New Zealand approach, rather than just look at things through the lens of one portfolio or another. Supplementary,
okay, okay, I'm going to go to Dr Deborah.
Supplementary, on
the hospital, we go to primaries, and if we've got some time for the subs, we'll get back to them. Dr Russell,

good morning, Minister, you said in your opening statement that the economy is clearly picking up. How does that? How is that consistent with inflation now going from 3.046% in the September quarter to 3.108% in the December quarter, where you've said, you know, frequently about the need to get inflation down. Clearly, it is down, but now it's picking up again. So can you just give us a little bit of an outlier, how you see
that

as being I think for context, let's remember at 3.1 it's less than half the 7.3 under the last government, we have had inflation in the target band for over a year. Under the last government, it spent three years outside the target band. So while this blip above target as unwelcome. It is our hope that this is only a quarterly change in the December quarter. Can I, if I can finish the answer, I have data to furnish for you with the increase in the December quarter that was largely driven by non tradable inflation, which is to say international factors, including International Air Transport and petrol This is likely. This is I'm reading you the advice from Treasury. This is likely transitory. That is very short term, with evidence of petrol prices easing in recent weeks in line with lower oil prices. The core inflation measure which Treasury advises me is the key underlying view of inflation in the economy has stayed at very at a very low level compared to history at more like 2.5% and that is the factor that is most significant. So yes, I prefer to see inflation and target, but I'm not concerned that we have at this point a major structural challenge. My reference to the economy growing stronger, of course, is in reference to what was by all economists and accounts an extraordinary result in the third quarter, with 1.1% growth well above what was being forecast by anyone

so Minister, I just find this is to be a bit of a fudge. You've frequently in the house talked about inflation figures coming down and taking responsibility for that, but now that they have actually gone up again in the last quarter. Do you take any responsibility for
that?

Well, I do take responsibility for the fact that domestic inflation, which is often referred to as tradable inflation, has declined significantly on our watch, and that is positive. I don't, I don't take full responsibility for that, because, of course, the Reserve Bank have the task of targeting inflation. What I take responsibility for is supporting them. That in that task in two ways, one by being careful about our own spending, but two by setting them it's actually the first piece of legislation we passed as a government, a very clear and explicit mandate to have a single target on inflation reduction. So we've supported the Reserve Bank to do its job here.

One of the interesting things minister is looking at the retail figures from December 2025 and if we dig into that, it's it's kind of interesting. They are down on December 2024 there was a brief surge in retail spending in November 25 but then it was a disappointing December for retailers. The interesting thing is that sitting in those figures, if we look closely, we see a number of things. One is that spending on clothing and on durables was significantly down, that spending on food was up, on consumables was up, that there was a greater proportion of spend put onto credit cards than onto immediate payment. So people were having to spend more, and they were having to put it on tick. And some of the inflation figures that were deeply worrying at that stage were things like as we've seen in the last year, food prices going up significantly. So up. Milk has gone up truly. The bottle of milk has gone up 15.8% a loaf of white bread has gone up 58 point. 3% meat has gone up 8.2% so it's starting to paint quite a worrying picture of what everyday life is like. Ordering New Zealand, it's putting off spending on durables, putting off spending on things that you can but having to spend a lot more just to eat. What is your reaction to that minister?

Well, look, our government is very focused on New Zealanders ability to meet the cost of living, and so I'm always concerned when New Zealanders are facing bigger price increases and essentials that they need to purchase for their families. I am pleased that those rates, for example, of food inflation, are nowhere near what they got to under the last of government, where we had double digit increases and it was completely out of control. But it is the case that we have seen that food prices, as they have internationally, picked up, that that activity has continued here. What we do expect to see is that our actions in terms of some of the components of overall the CPI basket, as it's referred to, will make a difference into the future. A key example of that a key driver of inflation increases has been rates increases by local government, and so I would love it if just overnight, we could stop that happening. But instead, we have put forward a programme of reform to both cap rates and reorganise local government to make it more efficient. And I hope colleagues around this table will join the government in supporting those actions, which are tangible reforms to address the underlying drivers of cost pressures for New Zealand households.
Okay,

I do need to carry on with this. So we've just had serious weather disasters through this country. We need to improve our resilience to weather disasters. We are putting a huge load onto local government to catch up with the underspend on infrastructure for decades, and yet, at the same time, this government is proposing to cap rates increases. How then do we expect local government to pay for the extraordinary investment in infrastructure that is needed

in two ways? The first is we expect local government to be extremely disciplined in its prioritisation of the resources that it invests that is essential. The second thing is that, as a government, we have been developing new tools for local government to use to fund the infrastructure that will require be required for growth. For example, Chris Bishop's work to replace development contributions with development levies to allow for new infrastructure funding and financing tools. For example, our local water done well reforms. So we do expect local government to continue to make significant investments in infrastructure. It's not our expectation that that will always require double digit rate increases, and if the member's position is that that is a sustainable position, while she's also concerned about the cost of living. I put to her that obviously those two positions are inconsistent.

I see your positions as being inconsistent. Minister. One last point, last point going back to inflation. If we look at the Half Year Economic and Fiscal Update from Treasury on page six, have the expected inflation figures for the next few years so. And in fact, the 2025 annual inflation was forecast to be 2.7% but of course, as we know it came up. And so what Reliance are you placing on those inflation figures, given that Treasury has missed by quite a margin in its forecasts in terms of preparing your budget policy,
I think the first thing you need to not over interpret quarterly data. So these Treasury forecasts refer to annual data. Well, that
was the annual rate from December to December.
So what I want to be clear about is that we have had
September to September was 3.01

yes, and so yes, there is some variation in the forecasts, but my confidence that inflation will remain in target is based on my confidence and the Reserve Bank's ability to use its monetary policy tools consistent with its mandate to reach its statutory requirement to keep inflation below 3% of the medium term, as is, as is in my in My remit to them. So
that will be increased interest rates then,

well, the alternative, which I think is what you're proposing, is a situation which the government, well, the government has taken significant steps to ensure that we put less pressure on inflation through our own spending position, this will be else being equal, provides room for the Reserve Bank to have lower interest rates than would otherwise be the case. Okay, we're
going to go to Dan bid. Wh,

thanks, Mr. Chair, thanks for coming today, Minister talk to us about investment boosts, because we've seen it's an immediate impact on. On revenue for this year. There's, you know, I guess there's a lag between the investment boost having a positive upside. But
what's,
what can you say around the forecast to the end of the period around the impact of investment boost? Yeah,

investment boost is a policy that is very much about building a more productive future, and I want to acknowledge colleagues on the side of the table who, when we announced that policy acknowledged its very strong rationale and the need for such a tax policy. Because what, what economists and experts have said for many, many years about the New Zealand economy is that one of the things that has grown out lower productivity growth rates than would otherwise be is the fact that our firms, our businesses, have lower levels of what's called capital intensity. That is, they're not making the investments and the capital that would allow them to grow faster in the future. So the question we asked ourselves was, how can we address that? How can we make sure that businesses are supported and encouraged to make the investments that will mean in future, we can have a faster, growing, more productive economy. The really clear advice from Treasury, from the ARD was the investment boost policy, allowing businesses to write off some of their tax costs if they make those investments was a very targeted way of achieving it. It it works if it's a permanent part of the New Zealand tax landscape. And so I would urge members around this table not to sacrifice it, because if we genuinely care about future productivity growth, then it is something that we need to keep going so that firms are getting a better tax position when they make

investments. Thank you. And one is as Eric recovery becomes underway this year, is it your expectation that investment boosts will start to have a greater upside? And you know, is Treasury given you any forecasts around that greater upside and what that looks like?

Yes, look, I understand everyone who's saying, well, what's the uptake been like? What are the numbers say? We, of course, don't get the numbers until people file their tax returns, so we just simply don't have that information yet. It's also very challenging for Treasury and the IRD to isolate what's the effective investment boost, because they can't compare the levels of investment that they have been that qualify for investment boost with what might have been, it's very difficult to know what would have occurred if we hadn't had investment boost in place. And so it is challenging to differentiate what if this investment has occurred because of investment boost and what would have occurred otherwise. So there are some challenges there. However, the IRD are going to provide me analysis and advice to look at the impact that it's had. What we can say is two things. On the one hand, firms have put to us that on the margin where they're not quite sure whether a business case stacks up, investment boost has allowed them to bring forward projects that they might not otherwise have occurred. I recall one of our MP spoke to a business who were going to expand their commercial kitchen because of investment boost. On the other hand, it's also the case that if businesses don't have the cash to make those investments right now, as realistically, many businesses don't, it doesn't matter that they'd get a tax write off. They still can't afford to make those investments. So it's not a cure to all ills, but when businesses are weighing up that decision, it can tilt them towards the investments we want them
to make. Okay, thanks, Dan. I'm going to have two quick questions from Dr woods and then Nancy Lu, thank you. And

just on that last question, Minister, I think it would be really useful, Mr. Chairman, if the committee could get a briefing when it's appropriate around the investment boost will be evaluated and to understand
the

discology behind it. Minister, you see budget 2026, is going to invest in important things. I think for many New Zealanders, having a job in security of work is one of the most important things. We saw unemployment in the last quarter, peak at around 5.5% with forecasts showing only a very gradual decline as we go through but one of the other numbers that was alarming, and that data was the the under utilisation of labour figure at 12.9% what initiatives will there be in budget 26 to give New Zealanders hope that the important thing of their job is going to be, and will be, will there be direct initiatives that will lead to being in work? Look,

can I just start by saying I absolutely agree with you, for the whole point for me, of having a growing economy is so that people have really good job opportunities. They can have job security, they have the possibility of a pay rise or promotion, that is the core reason why we want a growing economy. And as you know, when the economy grows faster, we have much lower levels of unemployment and much more employment. So monthly employment indicators of filled jobs for November are consistent with Treasury's view at the half year update of employment. Employment growth of 0.3% in the December quarter. So it remains the expectation that as the economy recovers, we will see that reflected both in lower levels of unemployment and also in higher rates of of employment. So that is positive initiatives, specific initiatives, one of the most important initiatives is our fast track legislation. That means that right now, there is work happening on the Auckland port with many, many people in a job that would otherwise not be occurring because instead it would be tied up and caught. It means that a housing development in Nelson is going ahead with many, many construction workers, many, many others hired that would otherwise be tied up in red tape across the country, whether it's quarries, whether it's housing development retirement villages that fast track legislation means the projects that would be sitting on the desk gathering dust are happening with real people, with real spades and real jobs, and so that is a very specific initiative that is supporting
employment. One stop

here. Thank you, Minister, and you talked about work that's currently underway, and yet we have these high unemployment numbers. So what I was looking for was specific initiatives you were looking for in budget 26 that will lead to more New Zealanders having that most important thing, a job, given that we've seen infrastructure peak bodies come out over the last week or so, and point to government decisions around housing decisions in Auckland leading to uncertainty in the construction sector and the inability to hire people because of government decisions forcing more uncertainty. So what are the initiatives in budget 26 that your government is going to put in place to ensure that more New Zealanders a have a job and B, more New Zealanders who are under utilised in the labour market or have access to more work.

Well, first of all, fast track will gain even more momentum, and more and more projects will be signed off, that's going to go gangbusters. So that's very exciting. Like everyone else, you'll have to wait for the budget to see the budget initiatives. But just to it is important, Mr. Chair, if you'll just give me a moment, I have latest data from Treasury addressing what we're seeing in terms of construction activity and employment activity. So of course, the quarter line, quarterly survey of business opinion from ng ie R shows businesses measures of their own activity rising materially. The survey of manufacturing and services has very positive momentum in the December month, and we are also seeing consents are ticking up and significantly lower interest rates now appear to be stimulating residential investment, and we are seeing that not only are consents growing, but there's an improving outlook for residential investment going forward, which shows improving net future expectations from firms about builders new orders. So we are seeing, as I've always said, will be the case as interest rates come down, some of those big construction projects are going to get underway again. You know, when interest rates got as high as they did under the last government, it was simply the case that developers who would otherwise have borrowed for a shopping mall complex or a big project couldn't make it straight up, because it was too expensive. If interest rates have come down, they're dusting off those plans and they're getting going again, which is great to see also the case that our budget, as is outlined in the budget policy statement, will make significant investments in infrastructure, through the land transport network, in terms of health infrastructure, education infrastructure, and those are projects, of course, that support jobs.
Okay. Did you have one last

Minister You talked of falling interest rates being one of the key things, the assumptions underlying your growth trajectory. Have you had advice or modelling of what an increase in interest rates will do to those assumptions?

Well, it is the case that Treasury and their advice do, and in their budget documents do set out what their expectations are for interest rates, and that that is one of the factors that goes into their forecasts. I'll acknowledge on interest rates, the Reserve Bank does the heavy lifting through the official cash rate, and it's important that we support the Reserve Bank to do their job. Is also the case that international factors do have impacts on the yields as well for our bonds, and that those factors do remain outside of our control. And my most recent update, I was I was told that still, expectation for our bonds, sorry, demand for our bonds is still very strong. We're getting very good coverage. We're not seeing the big increases and the cost of our bonds that that you could, that could occur with global volatility. So that picture is looking positive. I have also, in both budgets, as I recall, been advised by Treasury that in the absence of our spending reductions, that interest rates would be higher, would be expected to be higher. And so, you know, I. Stand by the fact that spending in a disciplined way is important, not just for the books, but it actually means that everything is more affordable for everyone who wants to borrow money in this economy.
Nancy Li,

thank you. Good morning, Minister, you made in your opening remarks that the economy is clearly recovering. That was your comment. My understanding, with my accounting background, is that there is always a lag time from when we see it physically, seeing it in the books. So I want to understand from you what impact will the recovery, recovering economy have in your 2026 budget.

Yeah, it's a very good point. We have to get those basics in place first before we get growth happening. And there is, in all the economic literature and order of events, you know, get inflation stable. That means interest rates can come down. Once interest rates are coming down, that means investment activity and consumption activity increases, then you get the growth occurring, then you get unemployment falling. And we are clearly stepping through that sequence as a country, just to in terms of the timing of that recovery. Of course, as I mentioned, September quarter, GDP came in much stronger than expected. So that does suggest that the recovery that Treasury were already forecasting is underway, possibly a bit sooner and a bit stronger than they forecast at the half year update. But I do want to caution that there's been a lot of volatility in the recent quarter to quarter GDP numbers, and so we do need to take some caution in interpreting a single data point to mean that those levels of growth would be sustained at that level into the future, or that the recovery is going to be significantly stronger. We would need more data points over a number of quarters for that to be the case. However, it is the case that the current data supports Treasury's view that a recovery is underway and will strengthen over the forecast period.

Thank you. Just finally from me, 120 sleeps until budget 26 May the 28th four months away. Have you coined? Have you coined a Have you coined a term or a name for this budget minister? We've had the no BS budget in the past.
Have
you got a Have you got an initial pointing of a phrase for this budget that will sum

it up? If not, well, the member will have to wait and see, like everyone else, but I would put to him that you know what the budget is going to look like. It's going to be responsible. It's going to prioritise and focus on the things that really matter to New Zealanders, their families and our future as a country is going to involve the tough choices that are necessary after a sustained period of frankly profligate spending. It is going to be a budget. It's about living within our means and preparing for a much stronger future for the country. So if anyone around this table has a suggestion for a name that matches that description, I'm all ears.
A responsible budget. Thank you, Minister.
We appreciate
your time. Thank
you.

Okay, members, that now takes us to aifu or haifu, however, you want to announce that acronym. We've got the secretary here of the Treasury, and, of course, Mr. Bunny here as well. We've got an allocation here till 945 and then we'll have a much needed coffee break for everyone, I'm sure. So welcome Mr. Rennie. Welcome Mr. Bunny. We look forward to your discussion and briefing on haere as was released on the 16th

of December. Look I'll turn over to the committee. Thank you, Mr. Chair, and welcome to members. As you know, the forecasts were finalised late October, early November last year. So I thought just cover off what's how we read the data since, and the ministers obviously touched on this and some of her remarks, I mean, you know, I mean, I think in the big picture, what underpins our forecast is a relatively kind of robust recovery over the next few years. And the data flow in general is, is consistent with that, you know, and like any data flow, there's indicators that are strong or weaker at any one time. We think broadly that the data flow has been broadly consistent with our forecasts. I think what we'd say is, at the time of haifu, we saw the balance of risks around our forecasts being reasonably evenly balanced. I think what we'd say is, in the near term, we think some of the downside risks that we saw are reduced. We saw we saw the risk at Tai pai food that if we had persistently weak business and consumer confidence, that the forecast might be lower than we had in high. Food, I think the data flow gives us a bit more confident, confidence so far, that that risk is reduced, at least in the in the near term. And then the final point I'd make is, is, if anything, the global outlook, notwithstanding, the noise in the global outlook looks a little stronger than at haupu, and it really is a story about the, I suppose, the pervasiveness and size of the investment in AI, and how AI is spilling over into supporting consumption, particularly in the United States. And so those two things in combination mean that the global outlook, notwithstanding the very significant risks that we remain worried about, looks a little bit stronger, at least getting there too.
Okay, thank you. Questions, we'll go to the honourable Barbara Edmund.

Happy New Year. Ian, thank you very much for that sort of update since haere last year, I just do want to turn back to haifu for this question. And if not, when do we expect to see those elements as part of When Will we see it in the accounts from what year?
Okay, okay, so, so I think there's two parts. Obviously, the context is obviously the current context is the current government policy statement and the
National
Maryland
transport programme. So that's locked in for 2427 so to the extent that some of the government initiatives are covered by that programme, that funding is in up to 2627
forecasts

as you will recall, the government is now in the process through the Minister of Transport about preparing the next GPS, and the implications of that for the budget will be a key part of the discussions that most of the finance has with her colleague. So, so, coming to your point is obviously beyond that that's not in the forecasts as of now, except to the extent that we do have a capital allowance in the forecast. And particular pressures are obviously claim against
that.

So, so, but, but it's obviously not allocated, certainly for our years as of yet. So, you know, I, my expectation is that by the time we get to budget 26 there will be at least a bit more clarity about what that programme looks like for the next few years and the implications for forecasts.

Because one of the major issues the infrastructure and civil construction sector constantly say to both parliamentarians all the time, there's the uncertainty as there's a change of government. And you you know fully give discretion to every government to choose what projects they want to do is cover as part of the GPS or the National Land Transport fund. Right reprioritisations Or changes happen all the time, depending on the GPS. My worry, though, is that if you are only accounting for or saying part of the funding or part of the government's contribution, tool is only covered within say that two year period, effectively, a new government can come in. We can come in at the end of this year, open up the books and actually show that capex and out years past the forecast period, it's actually going to change our debt figures. How do you smooth that so that Parliament is aware and it's transparent about that, that Treasury is transparent about that, all land transport is transparent about that. We need to, we need to stop these surprises.

I think there's and I think there's a there's a few pieces to that. It's a really good question. If I start with the national and transport fund, I think there is a structural issue that governments need to come. With. So if you go back to pre covid, largely the revenues going into the National
Fund. It

was, it was broadly balanced, I think. And Mr. Bishop made this point in his speech late last year, I think in the current GPS period, I think the, if you like, hypothecated revenues only cover about 56% of of the spending. So to your point, how do you how does the the parliament give certainty? Well, we did have a mechanism which was called the National Land Transport Fund, and the idea that it was going to be broadly funded through time, because of where it's got to. I think there's a real challenge about actually, are we ever going to get back to the point
of balance,

of balance, if not, and recognition that transport funding is going to be competing against funding for schools, hospitals, defence, etc, if you like. I think that's that's a real, the real tension point that I think both both current government and whoever's government after after the election will need to grapple with, because I think the outcome that you're talking about about having greater certainty about what the programme looks like, accepting that there'll be reprioritisation for changes of government is a is a correct one. So I think there's a there's a sort of structural issue there that we need to deal with. I think more generally, I'd also say the idea about infrastructure certainty somehow being decoupled from the rest of the budget. You know, you can go so far, but not completely. I'll give you an example about obviously, if you decide to build, as we will, New Zealand the next decade, essentially replace a lot of our provincial hospitals in New Zealand. The reality is, is highly unlikely we're going to replace hospitals with like for like, they are likely to increase operating expenses for the health system. Now, that's not a capital that's not a capital discussion, it's also an operating discussion. And so therefore governments trade off what that means for health spending versus education, etc. So I think what I'd say is a degree of, I think we can do better as a system, particularly around, I think transport, but that involves some quite hard calls for government, as I think I indicated the committee before Christmas. I'm very mindful about some of these longer term risks popping up and having to be dealt with by whoever is in government in the next term. And so that is why we're thinking very actively as a treasury about how do we build on the disclosure, at least. You know, in haifu, we did begin to quantify some of these risks a bit more accurately. And we'll think a bit about particularly, how can we foreshadow, at least, the potential size of increases that will be either later in this decade or into the 2030s

would you be able to because, because I am concerned about this, right? You've set it out in haere that basically, unless you increase levies or find another way to basically increase funding into the National Land Transport fund, the projects that have been basically announced and signalled as underway or going to happen won't be funded. You said, what for just over 50% basically covers it from the fund. obviously, 56% the reference was to the current GPS, yeah, which finishes next year. to what extent was the modelling of the recovery, which, obviously the minister had spoken a lot about this morning in Hae you fu based on interest rates falling. And therefore, what risks are there, given recent inflation data?

So I mean, let's put the inflation so we were, we in Haifa, we were seeing 2.9% for the single quarter. So yes, 3.1 is higher, but it's not massively higher. We'd expect inflation to get up towards the ban. We thought we just done that. Near reality, it was just over. And as I think Dr Russell's saying, Our expectation for as we go through this year is that will come through. I think the point to make is that I think recovery is built on a couple of things. One is we've we've broadly got a solid, if not spectacular, World Economic Outlook. We've actually got relatively high terms of trade for New Zealand, notwithstanding that dairy prices in particular have come up for about the last six months. And the third element is the support of of monetary policy. Now, one sense, you would expect as the economy, and I think this is the comment members been making, other comments, one reason why interest rates have been so low is because the economy has actually been weak. So you would actually expect, as the economy strengthens, the interest rates will be tilting back towards, you know, neutral. And ultimately that's a decision for the policy committee to make. But coming to your point Kiri is actually as interest rates go back to neutral, that's not really constraining the economy relative to the position that we were in until roughly late in 24 when interest rates were markedly restrictive.

I guess just on the broader point of inflation, we know that the single biggest contributor to the annual increase in inflation, electricity prices rose at 12.2% just any reflections from Treasury on their understanding of our electricity sector and how the reliance of fossil fuels for firming contributes to those price spikes and to cost of living for New Zealanders.

Well, I think, I think the point is the energy, because it's not just electricity, it's gas as well. So there's, there's multiple things going on so and as we know, the understanding about gas reserves is is cranking down. And it's not surprising that gas prices have increased significantly for households, as they have for businesses. So that's just a relative pressure. It's happening. Electricity, a little bit more complicated. Obviously, a big part of the increase in the last year has been the pass through of trans powers agreement with the Commerce Commission about increasing distribution charges, and that's in the context of the investments that need to happen in the grid with a view towards decarbonisation and increased electrification. And then obviously there's, there's another part, which is more around some of the pressures that have been in wholesale market, which actually were more prominent actually in 2024 rather than 2025 so I just be careful about saying, well, all the all the electricity stuff, is related to issues about firming and risk.
It's
part of the picture. But I wouldn't necessarily say it's the only part of the picture necessary, the most important part of the picture,

but if I may cross reference this, because, as you say, there's a lot of moving parts of this, and I'd say structural reform is also necessary for my perspective. Well, you recognise these are mixed ownership models. So the companies have significant private ownership and largely purpose that government has seen these as commercial entities. And what you will see through time is as the market works through the performance of those companies deteriorates and improves. And you rightly pointed out what has happened in the near return.

Yeah. I mean, I guess just on that point. I mean, would there be, or how would a potential mandate, or legislative change to the mandate to clarify the public good element of the provision, the energy for. New Zealanders look like. Would that change the way that they operate with that

Well, I think, well, that's a that's a big policy issue. And you know what? I'd say, it's you. I wouldn't really want to talk about it in a hypothetical, depending of what it looks like. and obviously that perennial question of profit just on that, something that's not hypothetical, is we have had you before us, at the end of last year, where you were talking about the work that was underway, with regard to SOEs in particular.
