Hey, thanks, Mr. Chair. Thanks for coming before us. I just want to turn now to some of the assumptions. So labour productivity, the assumption is that it returns to the long term one per cent. I'm wondering whether Treasury's factored in the impact of, say, investment boost, the RMA reform, some of the big stuff that government's doing, and whether it's factored into these forecasts, and if not, whether Treasury's actually looking at factoring those in the future.

I think the point is, we're always alert to structural changes that are happening in economy driven by policy. It is very hard to attribute a policy change and then say that gives you, mechanically, you know, point 1.2% of of productivity we we've obviously done some work in context of investment boost. I think what I'd say
is, you

know, the 1% productivity assumption. I think there are risks around that. I'd say, if you just looked at a productivity growth over the last 10 years, you'd say we were significantly optimistic. I think we're trying to say, when you look at productivity, you look through a somewhat longer timeframe, and implicitly, we've got an assumption that the policy environment is being enhanced, or in a way that is is consistent with, with stronger productivity. So that's, that's what we do in the general and obviously, as any government makes decisions, whether it's on tax or resource manager or whatever, we try and figure out, you know what? What does that do to the productivity story?

Okay, great. I have another question. I found it very interesting. There's a statement in the back there that says you talked about the evenness of the balance of upside and downscales is about right or about even. And then there's a statement in there that said there's a plus, there's a 50% plus or minus chance that the projections are about 7.3 billion or more. And I guess clearly there's a lot of assumptions here. Clearly there's a lot of uncertainty. My question to you is, if, policymakers want more confidence over, you know, the plan to return to surplus in a particular year. You know, rather than a 50% confidence level, rather bring that up to 80% confidence, what is the kind of what is the plus or minus, and whether you it's not in this but whether Treasury has actually done some work around it to give minister of finance more confidence over if we stick to if these assumptions hold, if the forecasts hold, we will be in surplus by a particular year. But have you done
something?

So we are doing some modelling work. Because I think, I think the issue, one of the reasons why we're putting the fan chart, as we call it, into the document, is is to highlight the considerable uncertainty, particularly as you get further out in the projection period. And I think it's important that members understand, you know, the the limitations on economic science, about trying to shoot four years ahead with with precision, and it's why we we put it in there, and we are trying to do some modelling work. So if, if you know, a minister said, Look, I really. Want to be in surplus by day x, we'd say, well, actually, if you want an 80% probability, you probably want to be shooting for a surplus of such and such, so that actually you get to balance. So we want to have that technical capacity to do that. I think, more generally, I think there's a and look, it's a live debate in Treasury about, do you is it is a good thing for governments to have point targets around your fiscal objectives? So I want to be you know, given that uncertainty, particularly when you're going out several years, it may be more relevant shorter term, where the uncertainty is real, but less less pronounced about, how do you set your fiscal targets? You know, do you set yourself up for high risk of failure if you say they'll be in surplus, you know, three or four years out? So I think that is, that's a but on the other hand, governments won't have clarity about what they're aiming at. There's an expectation they provide that clarity. So there's, there's trade offs in both directions, but I think the uncertainty you talked about also, I think raises issues for governments about how do you set your fiscal objectives and the trade offs that can come with that.
Thank you. Okay, very good. Did you have a sup on

that? On Investment boost? I mean, one of the things that governments haven't always done well is the post evaluation of actually, we said investment boost is going to increase GDP by 1.5% over 20 years, but you both yourself and the minister said that there may be some difficulty in actually pinpointing that, that increase In capital spenders because of investment boost. Why is that difficult? Given companies do file annual accounts, and if they don't file an Annual account, they are required to do a financial statement summary, generally, in both account or the summary or their accounts, they have to account for depreciation. Why is that difficult to actually measure what the changes are from their financial year, 2425 to 2526 when investment boost kicked in? Or, well,
I think that's because of the comparison. You're trying to say is, if let's, let's take a year, let's take 2526
Yeah,

you're trying to say, and we will be able to say here is what company X or IR will be able to find out, what does Company X invest? And the difficulty though, is how we don't have the comparison for 2526 about what they wouldn't have invested in the absence of investment boost,
but you would have at least know. You'll

know you can sort of say, as you say, you can go back to a preceding year. But that doesn't give you, because if you think about for some of the engagements that we have with large companies, they have a medium term investment path that, yeah, that cycles through. So the 2526 for a particular company might be significantly higher, but it was always going to be significantly higher because of where they were in their their investment cycle. And it would be, I think, incorrect to attribute that to investment boost. So it's the it's the because you never know what the counterfactual is for the year that you're trying to do it. Having said that, our colleagues at IR are trying to the evaluation, I think we are trying to do and we're trying to do it, my understanding is IR trying to have an exercise which is giving them at least some qualitative information. And obviously, as we get to the point when companies file, we'll be able to get a stronger information base, but you're always dealing with the situation that we will never observe a particular year without investment boost, but that will always be observing the year with investment boost.

So I guess it's very similar failure to be able to say there are 21,000 families who are going to receive the full tax relief right package of $250 and the difficulty has been that it's I asked find it difficult to find those families, but at the same time, they managed to provide advice to ministers that it's 21,000 families, the same way that Treasury and IRD are providing advice to the Minister, that is 1.5% GDP growth over two years. How do you reconcile that? What measures have you been directed by the minister in order to actually measure that? Because otherwise they're just empty numbers, because we are committing, you know, whether it's us or whether it's minister, we will be committing these things to the public. But how do we have confidence if you're saying that, you can't actually pinpoint that that's a result of investment boost, but you, on the other hand, you can say it's going to increase GDP by this amount, by 20 years.

You have anything, I think it's what I'd say is, I don't think it's black and white about I think we'll be able to I think that over a period of years, it will become clearer the contribution that investment boost is making. Are we getting closer to that 1.5% over 20 years? I mean, I think that the hard issue is probably between now and probably next two, three years, where the information base is going to be, it's going to be much thinner, and it's going to be harder to make confident assertions, to be frank, either, either way. So that, I think, is more the challenge, not that I think we I think we'll have a stronger information base at some point. But I think, particularly, you know, engaging with whoever's government over the next three years, there will be limitations about what we can say between us and I R, yeah, we're certainly trying to do within, within the context of the data available, and in the ability we can push the analysis. We don't certainly want to come back to that, because certainly the current government is really interested about how it's how it's playing out.
Thank you.
Okay, very good. Who are we going to next? Tom, we're going to Deborah Russell. Deborah Russell,

Thanks, Ian, I just want to dig just hear a little bit more about that latest inflation figure. I take your point of points being made, that single data point. We do expect that over time, it's, we hope it'll get down below the target.
It's

was nevertheless worrying that it went up. And it's, I know you say 2.9 is not that different from 3.1 it's hovering about the band, but it's kind of never quite gotten down into the band. It didn't in the September quarter, and now it's gone back up. But I'm just, I'm worrying, worrying about what's driving that a bit, the food prices and consumables here in New Zealand. And I'm worrying too about that retail spend data we got from December. If that carries on, we would expect that inflation would fall, because there'll be less retail spending. It's just, but, you know, people have to spend, they have to buy food. You know, there's so I'm just going, this feels really
so
to me. So
I think, I think the point, I'd agree with you, and I think we're seeing it in New Zealand, as we are in other countries, is that, you know, inflation has been quite sticky,
yeah.

So, I mean, you see that moment in the UK, you see that in Australia, you see that in the US. And I think there's, there's a, there's a couple of pieces there. One is, you know, international commodity prices have not been helpful to households. So, you know, we've seen a big increase in in food prices now, obviously New Zealand, as a dairy producer, has a net terms benefited from, but that's obviously impacted very much on all of us as as consumers when we go to the supermarket. So I think that's, that's part of the story about the sort of the some of these international developments. But also I think, you know, I think globally, you know, we've, it's been hard work getting the non traders inflation
down,

and so that's very much why you're sort of seeing many central banks now. They've either the easing cycle has generally stopped in most countries. And you know, the active discussion is about when, when does, when does a return to more neutral interest rates start? And so, you know, next week, for example, there's quite a bit of debate about where the Reserve Bank of Australia will start moving rates up there. Obviously, you've seen that debate in New Zealand in terms of what happens either later this year or into early next year. So I think, I think there's, I think that one of the challenges for our colleagues at Reserve Bank, central banks generally, is understanding what is really driving some of the stickiness in inflation. Because, you know, consumers perceiving this, you know consumer confidence actually, not just in New Zealand, but global is is still pretty subdued relative to previous cycles. And it's not surprising, right? Because people are seeing some of these pressures and prices.
Okay, please keep

going a little bit more on this. The other thing that worried me in that that December sales. I mean, December is normally a good month for retail, that's the Christmas, Christmas, and that's when a lot of retailers rely on that spend to get them through the rest of the year. So I was a little bit worried about that spend being down unless people. Got clever and bought stuff Friday sales, or whatever. The other, it's hard to know, but the other bit that was worrying me, there was the extent to which it was credit card spending, rather people putting stuff on the tick, and that's going to bite. Now, of course, that then will drive inflation down, but you can see the pressures on households.
So I think, I think, I mean the first, first point to make is, look, you don't want to dismiss any month's data, but nor do I think you want to read everything into it. So compared with
normally,

quite well, you should be able to take account that in season adjustment rush. So, so compared to September 24 we're still looking at household spending durables, for example, being similar, point 3% higher. So, you know, yes, there was weakness, and in the context of, but in the context of quite a significant lift, at least in durables over the last year or so, I think the other point to make, and we'll see this as recovery plays out. Is, is there are continually important structural stories happening in the economy. And you're so you're going to see that with with retail. You know, you see the the increasing presence of offshore, you know, retailers that shines, te moves, Amazons, those, those that information you talked about Deborah didn't include sales that New Zealanders have bought from offshore retailers, let alone to retailers who choose to Locate in New Zealand but are competing with existing retailers, IKEAs or Costcos or whoever so, so, you know, you'll, you'll consider, continue to even if the the overall trend is up, you know, there's significant, you know, structural change still happening in in retail, and we need to be mindful of that. Okay, we're
just a sap from Baba.

You talked about Ian, you talked about international commodities. And we've seen a really interesting basically, gold is over $5,000 an ounce. US Dollars silver, I think for the first time, hit over 100 there is some murmuring I'm reading, both internationally and domestically, around the impact of that, around confidence in traditional monetary policy. Given your earlier comments around international uncertainty and what's in particular, some of the comments around the reserve with being the US dollar, Are you actively providing advice to the Minister about this? And if so, are you able to share any of your reflections around some of those? Because it's not just in the last week.

No, no, no. And look, I've so I think the general point is, I think, you know, globally, there is more questioning of of central bank independence, and I think that the Fed is clearly the most visible part of that. But there are also others. So if you see, for example, you know, comments that Mr. Farage in the UK is making about, you know, if he were Prime Minister, what a government led by Him would do around both the current governor of the Bank of England and and how the Bank of England makes decisions. So it's not just a Trump thing. There are tussles in Europe between, for example, the Italian government and the central bank about who owns their gold, because actually hold a lot of it and and, you know, who should, who should have it. So I think people are looking and seeing all of that and are saying, well, maybe the sort of fiat currencies aren't quite the big we thought it was, and so they're going into gold and silver. I think for us in New Zealand, the sort of advice that we're giving is both. That's obviously a risk, but also there's an opportunity for New Zealand, you know, both in terms of our strength of our institutions, that the more that you know, as I think there is in New Zealand, much more consensus around the focus of our central bank. You know, that is a real asset for our country. You know,
if we
play that, we look we look stable.
Yes, we
look predictable. We look competent.
And

therefore, if that is how we can collectively portray ourselves to the world, we become an attractive place for capital. We place attract place for talent. We are safe place for New Zealanders to come to think about resettling, you know, so it's a very important part of think the story, and it's reflected in our advice to ministers increasing this idea about. Economic security, and that the elements that make that is a really, probably more important part of our advice than you might have seen, you know, three or four years ago.

The commonality between those international examples that you've provided is that they're far right, right governments and the we absolutely agree we're a small country at the bottom of the world, and really, we really depend on international cooperation and international rules based order of law, you know, like, that's the only way New Zealand really survives. And again, is it an attractiveness for other investors? Do you believe? What are the, what are the factors you think that that you've seen across other those other jurisdiction that we should be holding on to as parliamentarians to ensure that we don't fall to that sort of similar position, where we're having a government fighting with the independent reserve bank or Federal Bank? What are sort of the, what are the sort of conditions, we should be trying to encourage or support as parliamentarians? Because I think it's an important
thing.

I think, I think partly it's, it's comes back to your extent about how can policy be developed and in ways that it actually buys and if not, because I don't think 100% unimental Everything is realistic. That's That's naive, right? And it's in the way of what you should see in the democratic society, but, but in the sort of more 8020 that actually, yeah, there's a, there's a consensus around how monetary policy should operate. There's a consensus about at least the direction about how fiscal policy is going to operate, albeit there will be shifts about how you get there, and nuances of priorities, I think getting agreement around issues that are important to people investing in New Zealand, like the broad shape of our tax system, the stability of our regulatory you know system, and how that looks like an attractive place to have capital, those are the things I would, I would look at, Because I think, you know, and certainly in the past year, I've had the opportunity to travel with the minister and talk to some investors that that's what people are seeing when they when they are already investing in New Zealand. And I ask them a question, why? It comes back to stability? It comes back to competence, and that's both are administrative competence and people like us Reserve Bank, to be frank, political competence. So people see that the people in power are saying, you know, well, yeah,
it really does.
So, so if there's, if there's at least a coherent story across the political divide about the things which people agree to agree on, well, at least agree to disagree on. That is really helpful, too.
Can I? Can

I just add one thing to your list? You sort of touched upon it, on policy development, the fact that New Zealand has a neutral public service, and we are still one of the few countries in the world that has a very strong, neutral public service. I think that's a really important fact that we should be supporting.

Okay, we're actually into our break time members. I'm happy to go to break for like 20 minutes and then come back, and I'm sure the Secretary will be happy to ask answer more questions about haifu. And then we go into our long term insights and our other he's here all morning disposal. Why don't we reconvene at 1015 1015
thanks. What can I get you? Megan, each. La,
oh, yeah, why
not?
Yeah.

Wednesday 28 January 2026 | Finance and Expenditure Committee

Budget Policy Statement 2026 and Half-year Economic and Fiscal Update 2025

Hon Nicola Willis, Minister of Finance

The Treasury

Iain Rennie CNZM, Secretary to the Treasury and Chief Executive
Chris Bunny, Deputy Secretary, Budget and Fiscal Performance
